title: Songs in Isolation III: Umm... Omm?
author: Rose, Sophie
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Text from YouTube description box: "Things are difficult, and sometimes you just have to breathe through it. Breathe through the stress of relationship breakdowns, the stress of COVID-19, things changing for the worse in the blink of an eye. These are the times when all you can do is just... do you." 

 This piece is a continuation of the Extended Vocal Techniques and throat-singing practice from my master’s degree and explores the impact of physical locations and virtual (electronic) locations on voice use. I wrote it in response to renewed encouragement on social media to meditate and create a sense of space and peace in one’s environment in July of Melbourne’s second large-scale lockdown. 

 I used layers of voice using overtone (filtering vocal formants by control of the tongue, oropharynx, and nasopharynx) and undertone (period doubling the fundamental pitch with the false vocal folds) singing. Om (from Indian religions and a common syllable uttered in meditation or associated with yoga) was the first layer and inspiration for the sonic journey. The minimal harmony and shifting overtones and fundamentals create a space that hangings with no actual resolution but with some tension that builds over the duration and fades without musically resolving. 

 The work aimed to develop a faux-meditation style piece of music that created a relaxing space with an edge to the sonic content that stepped outside the genre-specific confines. That is, the lack of traditional harmony and an increased dissonance in note selection. The piece is intentionally short, so the sonic environment is disrupted prematurely for the established meditation music parameters.

Text from YouTube description box: "Things are difficult, and sometimes you just have to breathe through it. Breathe through the stress of relationship breakdowns, the stress of COVID-19, things changing for the worse in the blink of an eye. These are the times when all you can do is just... do you." 

 This piece is a continuation of the Extended Vocal Techniques and throat-singing practice from my master’s degree and explores the impact of physical locations and virtual (electronic) locations on voice use. I wrote it in response to renewed encouragement on social media to meditate and create a sense of space and peace in one’s environment in July of Melbourne’s second large-scale lockdown.

Om (from Indian religions and a common syllable uttered in meditation or associated with yoga) was the first layer and inspiration for the sonic journey. The minimal harmony and shifting overtones and fundamentals create a space that hangings with no actual resolution but with some tension that builds over the duration and fades without musically resolving.
